stages: Brainstorm all factors that might change population size or makeup in a community
Sort factors into categories and discuss which have the biggest impact
Define what good descriptions of population factors should include
Create quality levels from basic identification to detailed explanations
student_prompts: What causes populations to grow, shrink, or change over time?
How would you explain these factors to someone who has never studied population?
What makes one explanation of population change better than another?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that show clear progression from listing factors to explaining connections between population dynamics and time.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to describe how population factors work.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some population factors with help but my descriptions need work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can describe some population change factors on my own but miss important details.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can describe how population growth, structure changes, migration, and natural increase affect population dynamics over time.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can connect population dynamics to bigger demographic theories or predict future population trends.

self_check_prompts: Did I explain how each factor affects population over time?
Can someone understand population dynamics from my description?

no_evidence: Provide examples of population changes in familiar communities
Guide student to identify one clear population factor
emerging: Ask student to explain their thinking about identified factors
Provide sentence starters for describing population changes
developing: Prompt student to add specific details about how factors work over time
Ask student to make connections between different population factors
competent: Challenge student to apply population dynamics to unfamiliar contexts
Ask student to consider broader patterns or theories

stages: Brainstorm different ways we see population data displayed in maps and charts.
Examine what makes each method useful or challenging to understand.
Discuss how geographic features might affect which method works best.
Create criteria for comparing these methods across different regions.
student_prompts: What different ways have you seen population shown on maps or in data displays?
When might a dot map be more helpful than a choropleth map?
How does the size or shape of a geographic area affect which representation method works best?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select population representations that clearly show the same geographic area using different methods and include both urban and rural regions.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to compare population representation methods.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some population representation methods with help but my comparisons need work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify different population representation methods but need to connect them better to geographic regions.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can compare different methods for representing population density and distribution patterns across geographic regions.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can evaluate which representation methods work best for specific geographic purposes beyond basic comparison.

self_check_prompts: Did I explain how each method shows population differently across geographic areas?
Can I justify why one method might be better than another for a specific region or purpose?

no_evidence: Provide examples of population maps and ask what the student notices about how population is shown.
Guide the student to identify one specific method used to display population data.
emerging: Ask the student to explain what they see in each representation without teacher prompting.
Have the student practice describing differences between two methods using geographic vocabulary.
developing: Prompt the student to connect their method descriptions to specific geographic features or regions.
Ask how the same population data might look different when the geographic scale changes.
competent: Challenge the student to consider which method would be most effective for a specific research question or audience.
Ask the student to predict what problems might arise when using each method in different geographic contexts.

stages: Students share what they know about how mountains, valleys, and rocks form.
Teacher introduces plate movements and rock cycles as key processes.
Students work in groups to match geological features with their formation processes.
Class discusses and refines criteria for explaining these connections clearly.
Students test the rubric by evaluating sample explanations together.
student_prompts: What do you think causes earthquakes and volcanoes?
How might rocks change over millions of years?
What would a complete explanation of landform creation include?
How can we tell if someone really understands these processes?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student explanations that show clear progression from basic identification to sophisticated connections between processes and outcomes.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to explain how geological processes work.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some plate movements or rock types with help but my explanations need work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can explain basic plate movements or rock cycle processes but struggle to connect them to landform creation.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how plate movements and rock cycles create Earth's geological features with accurate scientific understanding.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can connect tectonic and rock cycle processes to predict future geological changes or regional variations.

self_check_prompts: Can I trace the steps from plate movement to landform creation?
Do my explanations use accurate scientific vocabulary and concepts?

no_evidence: Provide visual models of plate boundaries and rock formation.
Start with one simple example like volcanic island formation.
emerging: Focus on correcting one key misconception at a time.
Use hands-on activities to demonstrate accurate processes.
developing: Guide students to explicitly connect processes to specific landforms.
Ask 'What happens next?' to extend their thinking through complete sequences.
competent: Challenge students to apply their understanding to unfamiliar geological settings.
Introduce time scales and regional variations in geological processes.

stages: Students examine geological maps and landforms to identify what they notice and wonder about landscape features.
Students sort geological evidence into categories and discuss what different rock types and erosion patterns might tell us.
Students create criteria for what makes a strong geological analysis by comparing weak and strong examples.
Students refine criteria into performance levels from basic identification to advanced prediction and comparison.
student_prompts: What patterns do you see in this geological map and how might they connect to the landscape?
How would you know if someone has done a thorough analysis of geological evidence?
What would it look like to go beyond just identifying features to making predictions about landscapes?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select geological maps and landform examples that clearly show the progression from simple feature identification to complex analysis connecting rock types, erosion processes, and landscape development.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to analyse geological evidence or landforms.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some geological features with support but my analysis needs improvement.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can independently identify geological features but struggle to connect erosion processes with how landforms develop.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently analyse geological maps, diagrams and observations to accurately identify how erosion and rock types shape landscapes.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can apply geological analysis to predict how landscapes will evolve or compare geological processes across different regions.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain the connections between rock types, erosion processes, and the landforms I observe?
Am I using evidence from geological maps and diagrams to support my analysis?

no_evidence: Provide a simple geological map with clear features and guide student to identify one obvious landform.
Use questioning to help student notice basic patterns in the geological evidence.
emerging: Focus on one clear connection between rock type and landform to build confidence in analysis.
Provide sentence starters or graphic organizers to structure geological observations.
developing: Guide student to trace specific erosion processes step-by-step and connect them to landform features.
Use before-and-after diagrams to help student visualize how erosion shapes landscapes over time.
competent: Challenge student to make predictions about landscape changes based on their current analysis.
Introduce comparative analysis by providing geological evidence from a different region.

stages: Brainstorm what students already know about rainforests and their locations
Examine rainforest images and data to identify key characteristics
Organize characteristics into categories of climate, structure, and location
Define quality levels from basic identification to detailed analysis
Test the rubric against sample student work
student_prompts: What makes a rainforest different from other forests?
How would you organize rainforest information from simple to complex?
What details would show deep understanding of rainforest biomes?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that clearly show progression from basic feature identification to detailed structural analysis with accurate global distribution knowledge.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to describe rainforest characteristics or distribution.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some rainforest features with help but my descriptions need more accuracy.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can describe basic rainforest characteristics on my own but need more detail about structure or distribution.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can accurately describe rainforest characteristics, structure, and global distribution with appropriate detail.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can connect rainforest characteristics to climate patterns and compare distribution with other biomes.

self_check_prompts: Have I included characteristics, structure, AND global distribution in my description?
Can I explain WHY rainforests are located where they are?

no_evidence: Provide rainforest images and ask what the student notices
Start with one simple characteristic like temperature or rainfall
emerging: Guide student to check accuracy using reliable sources
Help organize ideas into clear categories of characteristics, structure, and location
developing: Prompt for specific details about rainforest layers or precipitation amounts
Ask where rainforests are found globally and why those locations make sense
competent: Challenge student to explain connections between climate and rainforest characteristics
Encourage comparison with other biome types and their distributions

stages: Students brainstorm what they already know about rainforest destruction and its effects.
Teacher guides students to categorize their ideas into causes and consequences.
Students examine the difference between identifying impacts versus analyzing connections between them.
Class develops criteria for what makes analysis complete versus incomplete or shallow versus deep.
student_prompts: What happens when rainforests are cut down and why does this occur?
How can we tell the difference between listing facts and analyzing connections?
What would make our analysis more complete or thorough?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that clearly show different depths of analysis from simple listing to complex cause-effect reasoning.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to analyse how deforestation affects rainforest ecosystems and communities.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some causes or consequences of deforestation with help.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify causes and consequences of deforestation on my own.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can analyse multiple causes of deforestation and explain how they affect rainforest ecosystems and communities.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyse deforestation impacts at different scales and evaluate possible solutions.

self_check_prompts: Have I explained the connections between causes and their effects rather than just listing them?
Have I considered impacts on both ecosystems and human communities?

no_evidence: Provide a specific deforestation scenario to analyze.
Ask what might happen when trees are removed from rainforests.
emerging: Guide student to identify one clear cause-effect relationship.
Prompt student to consider both human and environmental impacts.
developing: Ask student to explain how their identified causes connect to specific consequences.
Encourage student to consider multiple stakeholder perspectives.
competent: Challenge student to consider impacts at local and global scales.
Ask student to evaluate which solutions might be most effective.

stages: Students brainstorm what makes their neighborhood different from other places they know.
Teacher introduces the term 'region' and students discuss what physical and human features might be shared across larger areas.
Students examine maps and images to identify patterns of shared characteristics.
Class collaboratively defines what it means to 'identify regional characteristics' and creates success criteria.
student_prompts: What physical features do you notice that are the same across this area?
What human activities or ways of living do people in this region share?
How do these shared characteristics make this region different from others?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that clearly show progression from listing isolated features to connecting shared characteristics across a defined regional area.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to identify what makes a region special.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some features of places with help but don't see how they connect to regions.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify some physical and human characteristics but struggle to show how they define a region.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently identify the shared physical and human characteristics that define a specific region.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can identify regional characteristics and explain how they create a unique regional identity.

self_check_prompts: Did I identify both physical features and human activities that are shared across the region?
Can I explain how these shared characteristics make this region distinct from others?

no_evidence: Provide a simple map or image and ask what the student notices about the area.
Guide the student to point out one obvious feature they can see.
emerging: Ask the student to compare two similar features they identified and discuss what they have in common.
Introduce the concept that regions are areas where people and places share similar characteristics.
developing: Help the student group their identified characteristics into physical and human categories.
Guide them to explain how these characteristics are shared across the entire regional area.
competent: Challenge the student to explain why these shared characteristics make the region unique.
Ask them to predict what might happen if one of these key characteristics changed.
